narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a white male applicant with a solid upper-middle-class family background, you've put significant effort into your law school journey, including scoring a commendable 38 on your LSAT and planning to attend full-time. However, the admissions model, which predicts the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try based on profiles like yours from past students, estimated a 74.7% chance that you would fail it—far higher than what it sees in successful applicants. This high predicted risk is why your application was rejected; the school uses this model to prioritize those with stronger signals of bar passage success. The most influential factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 2, which is notably lower than the typical student's 5.53 average and signaled weaker performance relative to peers. Your Year 1 decile of 3, also below the average of 5.71, reinforced this concern as the next biggest influence in the same direction. On a brighter note, your LSAT score stood out positively, above the dataset average of 36.70, helping pull the prediction somewhat toward passing success. Your undergraduate GPA of 2.80, however, tipped things further toward risk since it's below the typical 3.21, though less strongly than your deciles. Smaller positives came from your family income bracket and full-time enrollment plans, which aligned well with successful students but weren't enough to offset the academic concerns. Compared to typical admitted students, your deciles place you in the lower performance tiers, while your LSAT is a strength that many would envy. To move forward, consider retaking courses or programs to boost your decile rankings, perhaps pursuing post-baccalaureate options to lift your GPA, and reapplying after building a stronger academic profile. You're clearly capable, especially with that LSAT, and targeted improvements could shift the model's view dramatically. Keep pushing—you've got this.
